clean_story: Light bending tricks.  Objects vanish, unseen. "Metamaterial cloaking" uses engineered structures—smaller than light waves—to reroute rays like water flowing around a rock. No magic. Just physics exploiting how light avoids what it can’t touch. Normal materials reflect or absorb light. Metamaterials twist it. Their nano-patterns—often gold or silicon—act like tiny antennas, steering light in unnatural curves. Example:A 2006 Duke University cloak hid a copper cylinder from microwaves. Later versions worked on visible light—but only for narrow colors, like a red apple disappearing under green laser. Limitations?Speed. Light slows inside the cloak, creating a lag. And perfection demands atomic precision—one flaw, and shadows leak. Three related subjects are light manipulation, optical illusions and stealth tech.
